lessons.title: Variables, Constants & Type System

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Variables, Constants & Type System matters because value declaration defines mutability, inference, and how explicit your api contracts are.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain when immutable bindings are the default choice and when an explicit type is clearer than inference.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Using mutable state by default makes data flow harder to reason about and easier to break during refactors.

code_examples.title: Variables, Constants & Type System In Practice
code_examples.explanation: Explain when immutable bindings are the default choice and when an explicit type is clearer than inference.

key_takeaways: Value declaration defines mutability, inference, and how explicit your API contracts are.

lessons.title: Null Safety (sound null safety, ?, !, late, required)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Null Safety (sound null safety, ?, !, late, required) matters because nullability makes absence explicit and forces safe access before a value is used.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Describe the safe unwrapping path, the sharp edges of force access, and why explicit optionality improves correctness.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Teams introduce crashes when they bypass null checks instead of designing the API around missing state.

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Functions (named params, optional params, arrow functions, typedefs) matters because function design shapes readability, call-site clarity, and how logic is composed across a feature.

content.body: Be ready to talk about parameter labels, closure behavior, and why small, composable functions are easier to test.

content.heading: Common Mistake

lessons.title: Control Flow (if, switch expressions, loops, pattern matching)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Control Flow (if, switch expressions, loops, pattern matching) matters because control flow determines how a program branches, exits early, and handles exhaustive cases.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain why explicit branches and early exits are usually easier to maintain than deeply nested conditionals.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Unclear branch conditions create dead paths, duplicate logic, and hidden edge cases.

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Async Programming (Future, async/await, Stream, StreamController, Completer) matters because concurrency is about coordinating work safely so the ui stays responsive while shared state remains correct.

content.body: A strong answer explains structured tasks, cancellation, isolation, and how work returns to the main UI boundary.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Background work becomes dangerous when cancellation, ownership, and main-thread updates are not explicit.

lessons.title: BuildContext (what it is, how to use it correctly)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: BuildContext (what it is, how to use it correctly) matters because context objects expose location-sensitive information such as environment, ancestry, and presentation state.
content.code_snippet:
final theme = Theme.of(context);
final router = GoRouter.of(context);

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Be ready to explain what the context can safely read and why using it after disposal or outside scope is dangerous.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Storing a context past its valid lifetime creates bugs that look random at runtime.

lessons.title: Responsive Design (MediaQuery, LayoutBuilder, Platform checks)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Responsive Design (MediaQuery, LayoutBuilder, Platform checks) matters because responsive design adapts structure and spacing to the real device constraints instead of assuming one screen size.
content.code_snippet:
final compact = MediaQuery.of(context).size.width < 600;
return compact ? mobileView() : tabletView();

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain the breakpoint or sizing signal you use and how content priority changes on compact versus regular screens.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Hard-coded dimensions make the UI fragile across orientation, split view, and accessibility sizes.

key_takeaways: Responsive design adapts structure and spacing to the real device constraints instead of assuming one screen size.

lessons.title: Platform Channels (MethodChannel, EventChannel, Pigeon)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Platform Channels (MethodChannel, EventChannel, Pigeon) matters because platform integration bridges framework code with host apis that are not exposed by default.
content.code_snippet:
const channel = MethodChannel("camera");
final granted = await channel.invokeMethod<bool>("requestPermission");

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain how data crosses the boundary, how failures are surfaced, and how you keep the bridge testable.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Thin wrappers become unmaintainable when they leak platform details through the entire app.

lessons.title: Accessibility (Semantics, screen readers)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Accessibility (Semantics, screen readers) matters because accessibility makes the same feature understandable and operable for users with different sensory and motor needs.
content.code_snippet:
Semantics(
  label: "Open profile",
  button: true,
  child: Icon(Icons.person),
)

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain semantic labels, focus order, dynamic type, and how you test assistive flows instead of assuming them.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Accessibility breaks when semantics are added as polish instead of being part of the feature definition.

lessons.title: Optionals (?, !, optional binding, optional chaining, nil coalescing, guard let)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Optionals (?, !, optional binding, optional chaining, nil coalescing, guard let) matters because nullability makes absence explicit and forces safe access before a value is used.
content.code_snippet:
var nickname: String?
let label = nickname ?? "Anonymous"
print(label)

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Describe the safe unwrapping path, the sharp edges of force access, and why explicit optionality improves correctness.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Teams introduce crashes when they bypass null checks instead of designing the API around missing state.

lessons.title: Property Wrappers (@State, @Binding, custom wrappers)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Property Wrappers (@State, @Binding, custom wrappers) matters because property wrappers package state access rules so usage sites stay small and consistent.
content.code_snippet:
@State private var query = ""
@Binding var isPresented: Bool

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Talk about ownership, projected values, and why wrappers should clarify state instead of hiding it.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Wrapper-heavy code becomes confusing when the ownership model is no longer obvious from the declaration.

@Binding var isPresented: Bool

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Sheets, Alerts, Confirmations, Popovers matters because transient presentation apis show short-lived work without losing the context of the current task.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Discuss presentation ownership, dismissal flow, and how you keep temporary state from leaking into long-lived models.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Presentation bugs appear when multiple features think they own the same modal state.

code_examples.explanation: Discuss presentation ownership, dismissal flow, and how you keep temporary state from leaking into long-lived models.

key_takeaways: Transient presentation APIs show short-lived work without losing the context of the current task.

lessons.title: Animations & Transitions (withAnimation, matchedGeometryEffect, .transition, PhaseAnimator, KeyframeAnimator)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Animations & Transitions (withAnimation, matchedGeometryEffect, .transition, PhaseAnimator, KeyframeAnimator) matters because animation communicates state change, spatial relation, and feedback when it is tied to a real interaction.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain what drives the animation, how identity is preserved, and when restraint is better than spectacle.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Motion becomes noise when it is added without a state change or without respecting performance limits.

lessons.title: Drawing (Shape, Path, Canvas, custom shapes)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Drawing (Shape, Path, Canvas, custom shapes) matters because custom drawing gives you control when standard components cannot express the visual model you need.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Talk about coordinate systems, invalidation cost, and when a custom renderer is justified over simple composition.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Custom drawing becomes expensive when you redraw too much or mix rendering with unrelated view logic.

lessons.title: Gestures (TapGesture, DragGesture, MagnificationGesture, simultaneous/sequenced)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Gestures (TapGesture, DragGesture, MagnificationGesture, simultaneous/sequenced) matters because gesture systems translate raw input into deliberate interactions without conflicting with the rest of the interface.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain hit testing, gesture priority, and how to combine gestures without making the UI unpredictable.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Gesture-heavy screens break down when interaction rules are implicit or when accessibility alternatives are missing.

lessons.title: Data Flow & Architecture (MVVM, environment, preferences)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Data Flow & Architecture (MVVM, environment, preferences) matters because data flow describes how state, dependencies, and user intent move through a feature over time.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Talk about unidirectional flow, environment injection, and how explicit boundaries reduce surprise updates.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: When writes can happen from everywhere, debugging turns into hunting invisible side effects.

lessons.title: SwiftData (models, queries, relationships, migration)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: SwiftData (models, queries, relationships, migration) matters because persistence stores state safely across launches while keeping write policy and migration risk explicit.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Discuss the right storage layer for the job and how schema changes are handled over time.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Using the simplest store for every problem usually creates migration pain or weak query performance later.

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Networking (URLSession, async/await, Codable, JSONDecoder, error handling) matters because error handling defines how failures are surfaced, recovered from, and translated into user-safe outcomes.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Talk about explicit failure paths, domain-specific errors, and when to propagate versus recover locally.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Silent catches and generic error types make production debugging much slower.

content.body: Combine (Publishers, Subscribers, operators — enough to understand legacy code) matters because reactive pipelines model asynchronous streams so transformation, cancellation, and side effects stay explicit.

lessons.title: App Intents & Shortcuts

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: App Intents & Shortcuts matters because platform services expose system-level features such as shortcuts, widgets, and notifications to the product surface.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain the lifecycle, permission model, and what state has to remain available when the system invokes the feature later.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: These integrations fail when app state is assumed to exist instead of being reconstructed safely from inputs.

lessons.title: Keychain & Security

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Keychain & Security matters because security is about reducing blast radius: protect secrets, validate trust boundaries, and assume hostile conditions.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Strong answers mention transport security, secure storage, least privilege, and how sensitive data is rotated or revoked.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Security work fails when secrets live in source control or when trust is delegated to the client alone.

code_examples.title: Keychain & Security In Practice
code_examples.explanation: Strong answers mention transport security, secure storage, least privilege, and how sensitive data is rotated or revoked.

content.body: Performance (Instruments, view identity, structural identity) matters because performance work removes wasted work from the critical path so the product feels immediate under real load.

lessons.title: Data Structures (Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue, Hash Table, Tree, Binary Search Tree, Heap, Graph)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Data Structures (Array, Linked List, Stack, Queue, Hash Table, Tree, Binary Search Tree, Heap, Graph) matters because data structures trade memory, ordering, and operation cost to fit different workloads.
content.code_snippet:
Array -> fast indexed read
Queue -> FIFO work
Hash table -> fast key lookup

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: System Design Basics (load balancing, CDN, microservices, message queues, database sharding) matters because system design is about choosing boundaries and bottlenecks intentionally before scale forces bad defaults on you.
content.code_snippet: Client -> CDN -> API -> Queue -> Worker -> Database

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Discuss load distribution, state ownership, failure modes, and the first scaling move you would make.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Design answers feel shallow when they jump to microservices before naming the real bottleneck or constraint.

code_examples.title: System Design Basics (load balancing, CDN, microservices, message queues, database sharding) In Practice
code_examples.code: Client -> CDN -> API -> Queue -> Worker -> Database
code_examples.explanation: Discuss load distribution, state ownership, failure modes, and the first scaling move you would make.

key_takeaways: System design is about choosing boundaries and bottlenecks intentionally before scale forces bad defaults on you.
Discuss load distribution, state ownership, failure modes, and the first scaling move you would make.
Design answers feel shallow when they jump to microservices before naming the real bottleneck or constraint.

lessons.title: Security (OWASP mobile top 10, HTTPS, data encryption, secure storage, code obfuscation)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Security (OWASP mobile top 10, HTTPS, data encryption, secure storage, code obfuscation) matters because security is about reducing blast radius: protect secrets, validate trust boundaries, and assume hostile conditions.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Strong answers mention transport security, secure storage, least privilege, and how sensitive data is rotated or revoked.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Security work fails when secrets live in source control or when trust is delegated to the client alone.

lessons.title: Agile & Scrum (sprints, ceremonies, story points, kanban)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Agile & Scrum (sprints, ceremonies, story points, kanban) matters because agile practices are coordination tools for planning, feedback, and adaptation under uncertainty.
content.code_snippet: Backlog -> Sprint planning -> Daily sync -> Review -> Retro

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain how the ceremony changes team behavior, not just what the meeting is called.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: A process becomes wasteful when it is performed mechanically without improving planning or feedback quality.

lessons.title: Code Quality (code review best practices, linting, static analysis, tech debt)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Code Quality (code review best practices, linting, static analysis, tech debt) matters because design principles keep code readable by pushing complexity behind clear names, boundaries, and tradeoffs.
content.code_snippet:
Rule: one reason to change per type
Smell: giant class with UI, networking, and persistence together

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain the principle with a concrete feature example instead of reciting the acronym alone.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Principles become dogma when they are applied without checking whether the code actually became simpler.

lessons.title: Clean Code (naming, functions, KISS, DRY, YAGNI, comments)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Clean Code (naming, functions, KISS, DRY, YAGNI, comments) matters because function design shapes readability, call-site clarity, and how logic is composed across a feature.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Be ready to talk about parameter labels, closure behavior, and why small, composable functions are easier to test.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Long parameter lists and hidden capture behavior make business logic harder to read and debug.

lessons.title: Accessibility (WCAG, a11y in mobile, VoiceOver, TalkBack, Dynamic Type)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Accessibility (WCAG, a11y in mobile, VoiceOver, TalkBack, Dynamic Type) matters because accessibility makes the same feature understandable and operable for users with different sensory and motor needs.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain semantic labels, focus order, dynamic type, and how you test assistive flows instead of assuming them.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Accessibility breaks when semantics are added as polish instead of being part of the feature definition.

lessons.title: Internationalization & Localization

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Internationalization & Localization matters because internationalization prepares the product for multiple languages and regional formats before strings start multiplying.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: A solid answer covers externalized strings, pluralization, formatting, and why layout must survive text expansion.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Embedding user-visible copy in feature code makes localization late, expensive, and error-prone.

lessons.title: App Performance (profiling, memory leaks, lazy loading, image optimization)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: App Performance (profiling, memory leaks, lazy loading, image optimization) matters because performance work removes wasted work from the critical path so the product feels immediate under real load.

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Explain how you measure first, what you optimize second, and how identity, rendering, memory, and I/O all interact.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Micro-optimizing without measurements usually hides the real bottleneck and makes the code harder to change.

code_examples.title: App Performance (profiling, memory leaks, lazy loading, image optimization) In Practice

lessons.title: Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files)

content.heading: Core Idea
content.body: Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files) matters because dependency management keeps builds reproducible while controlling upgrade risk and package sprawl.
content.code_snippet: package-lock.json keeps the resolved graph stable

content.heading: What Interviewers Expect
content.body: Talk about version pinning, lock files, security review, and what belongs in the app versus in a shared package.

content.heading: Common Mistake
content.body: Unpinned dependencies create non-reproducible builds and surprise regressions.

code_examples.title: Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files) In Practice

key_takeaways: Dependency management keeps builds reproducible while controlling upgrade risk and package sprawl.
Talk about version pinning, lock files, security review, and what belongs in the app versus in a shared package.
Unpinned dependencies create non-reproducible builds and surprise regressions.

interview_questions.title: Variables, Constants & Type System · Conceptual
interview_questions.question: Explain Variables, Constants & Type System to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Value declaration defines mutability, inference, and how explicit your API contracts are. Then connect it to production behavior: Explain when immutable bindings are the default choice and when an explicit type is clearer than inference. interview_questions.question: Explain Null Safety (sound null safety, ?, !, late, required) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Nullability makes absence explicit and forces safe access before a value is used. Then connect it to production behavior: Describe the safe unwrapping path, the sharp edges of force access, and why explicit optionality improves correctness. Finally, name the failure mode you are trying to avoid: Teams introduce crashes when they bypass null checks instead of designing the API around missing state.

interview_questions.question: Explain Dependency Management (CocoaPods, SPM, pub.dev, versioning, lock files) to a teammate who knows the platform but has not used this concept directly.
interview_questions.model_answer: Begin with the core idea: Dependency management keeps builds reproducible while controlling upgrade risk and package sprawl. Then connect it to production behavior: Talk about version pinning, lock files, security review, and what belongs in the app versus in a shared package.
